Since getLeavesData is a Map, this code will fail if the SAME leaf key is used more than once, e.g.
  '//author[@FirstName="Jane" or @FirstName="John"]'

It will produce invalid SQL:
  SELECT * FROM FRAGMENT WHERE anchor_id = ? AND xpath ~ ? AND  attributes @> '{"FirstName":"John"}' OR

  o.h.engine.jdbc.spi.SqlExceptionHelper   : SQL Error: 0, SQLState: 42601
  o.h.engine.jdbc.spi.SqlExceptionHelper   : ERROR: syntax error at end of input

This would need changes to the cps-path-parser to fix.

This method needs to be updated too!
Perhaps it would be easier to refactor both methods together, since they only differ by anchorId condition.

Please add a test case for using the same leaf key, e.g.
  'same leaf key with or operator' | '//author[@FirstName="Jane" or @FirstName="John"]' || ["/shops/shop[@id='1']/categories[@code='2']/book/author[@FirstName='Jane']", "/shops/shop[@id='1']/categories[@code='2']/book/author[@FirstName='John' and @Surname='keil' ]"]

Note: code will fail in this case.
After discussing with Toine, we agree duplicate leaf key is not a blocking issue. A separate story can be done later to add support for duplicate leaf keys if needed.
After discussing with Toine, we agree this is not a blocking issue now. The code can be merged without this. A separate story can be done later to add support for duplicate leaf keys if needed.

Since we are using QueueBooleanOperators in OperatorCondition ,In case of ancestoraxis   "ancestor that is a list with compound key" fails beacuse when cpspath is queried it intiallz assumes as normalizedpath until processingancestoraxis becomes true,once it recognise the ancestor tries to get the attributes ,operator but the  QueueBooleanOperators is empty and get null operator. So, added different condition
I agree with Luke, we should not have two different methods, since appendCondition only supports AND.
Agreed. This method only supports AND but not OR. I'm guessing this will cause problems, but I can't verify right now because of the bug in FragmentQueryBuilder.java:76

Because of operator precedence in SQL, we need to bracket the leaf conditions check.
Example:

Query for Smith:
  //author[@Surname="Smith"]

returns 1 author (Joe Smith):
  /shops/shop[@id='1']/categories[@code='2']/book/author[@FirstName='Joe' and @Surname='Smith']

Query for author of Dune:
  //author[@title="Dune"]

returns 1 author (Joe Bloggs):
  /shops/shop[@id='1']/categories[@code='1']/book/author[@FirstName='Joe' and @Surname='Bloggs']

But query for Smith or Dune:
  //author[@Surname="Smith" or @title="Dune"]

returns 3 authors! (1 duplicated)
  /shops/shop[@id='1']/categories[@code='1']/book/author[@FirstName='Joe' and @Surname='Bloggs']
  /shops/shop[@id='1']/categories[@code='2']/book/author[@FirstName='Joe' and @Surname='Smith']
  /shops/shop[@id='1']/categories[@code='2']/book/author[@FirstName='Joe' and @Surname='Smith']

Looking at the SQL:
    SELECT * FROM FRAGMENT 
    WHERE
        anchor_id = ? 
        AND xpath ~ ? 
        AND attributes @> '{"title":"Dune"}'
         OR attributes @> '{"Surname":"Smith"}'

I think the attribute conditions need to be bracketed, like this:
    SELECT * FROM FRAGMENT 
    WHERE
        anchor_id = ? 
        AND xpath ~ ? 
        AND (attributes @> '{"title":"Dune"}'
         OR attributes @> '{"Surname":"Smith"}')
Note: in above, the "duplicate" author is coming from a different anchor. Without brackets, the OR in SQL will skip the anchor_id and xpath checks, giving incorrect results.
